conceptually, stick with me conceptually, you've now got buyers for, and I'm sorry, I missed another key piece, is, you know, this buyer's list, you know, now you've got emails going out to the buyer's list, just follow up emails, and then somewhere in there, you might tell them about the herbal kit. Now one of the things about buyer's lists is in my experience, someone on your buyer's list is worth 15 to 20 times what someone on your prospect list is worth. So just, if you often, like I would do a launch back in my stock market days, I'd have, I'd have 5,000 people on this prospect list, and then I would have 500 people on my buyer's list in the early days. And I would do a promotion, come up with a new product, and I would do more sales to this 500 person list than to that 5,000 person list. ## [06:06 - 09:10]

getting emails, you know, a couple times a month, and you're going to keep up that publication schedule. You don't want to just start hammering in with all these emails for your upcoming launch. So generally you'll create that launch list, and then you'll encourage people to move over from this list to this list. And when I say encourage them, that means you're going to mail them, send them to a squeeze page. A squeeze page offers some cool things, some cool videos, cool bonuses, cool whatever, but that leads them into this sequence. Generally with these launch lists, they're sort of an episodic type thing. When someone goes on your launch list, I'm emailing them through the launch sequence, but I'm not, you know, after that launch sequence is over, I'm usually not mailing that list again because they're still over on this list. The other thing is, is if you move them over to this launch list here, and you send a whole bunch of emails, and usually during a launch you do send a bunch of emails, maybe at some point they decide that they're not interested, they can unsubscribe, but they'll only be unsubscribing from this list. Now, if they want to unsubscribe from all your lists, then they can do that as well, but generally they'll unsubscribe from this list, but they'll still be on your main list or on your buyer's list. This is how you can be basically doing a launch. Like, you know, in most of my businesses I'll do them three or four times a year, but I know people that are doing them every single month doing a launch every month. Well, how do you do a launch to your prospect list every month? If each one of these dots were a month, and then all of a sudden you send them a whole bunch of emails here, and then a whole bunch of emails here, and then a whole bunch of emails here for each different launch, you might have some list fatigue. But instead what you do is you give them the opportunity to join these launch lists and take part in that launch and get the great pre-launch content that you're going to put out, and this gives you the opportunity to continue to launch after launch after launch, not burn out your list, not burn out your buyer's list, and just keep on rolling along. So this is, you know, and then you come up with an idea for another product, you just create another launch list and another sequence, and you're sending them over. Now, if you're just starting out, when John did his first launch, he didn't come up with a separate launch list. He just did his full-out launch, this is his first launch, did the full-out launch to his full list. But in general, you typically want to do a new launch list when you're coming out with a new product, especially if it's somewhat of a departure from what you've done in the past. You know, they bought the board game, they're getting a newsletter, but that board game might be really different from the home study course. The home study course might be on, you know, flower essences or something, which is related and perfect and a great fit for his list, but slightly different. So maybe not everyone here is interested in flower essences, so you put them over on that list for people that are interested in that flower essence launch.

## [09:10 - 10:20]

So that is how you do it. That's how you orchestrate this without burning out your list, and this is how you continue to build that list relationship. Every business will be different. You know, how you organize your list will be different. It will evolve over time. Another one final piece is when you get people to go through this opt-in process and opt-in for that new list, that reinvigorates the lead, the lead being the person on the list. In other words, you know, when someone first joins your list, it's the best time, they're the most responsive when they first join your list. And then over time, on average, they get less responsive. They see a bunch of your emails, they get less responsive over time. That's just a rule of lists that goes back 100 years. But when you get them to step up an opt-in again, that re-warms them up and that really reinvigorates the list. So it is generally a great thing when you're going into a launch for a new product to create that new launch list. So that's sort of the organization of lists within the business launch formula. Again, lots of different ways you can do this, but conceptually this is how it goes. Now I'm going to tell you, I'm going to give you a couple more examples in the next video.
